summary:
The core pathology—surfactant deficiency leading directly to diffuse pulmonary atelectasis—is well-established across all retrieved materials. This results in a loss of compliant lung tissue due to collapsed units; thus, less total volume remains available during normal breathing cycles compared to what’s needed before closure begins—which means FRC becomes lower than closing volume.

Final decision based strictly on new data review:

\boxed{\text{A}}
The condition arises primarily because of deficient surfactant causing alveolar collapse (atelectasis). As a result, the amount of air retained post-exhalation decreases significantly—the functional residual capacity falls beneath the level required to keep airway openings patent, hence making FRC less than closing volume.
